header.stance: Favor front-end and intermediate duration; selective in credit and cautious on the long end amid oil and supply risk.
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The July 14 New York close delivered a sharp reversal in the market’s most immediate fear without resolving the larger inflation problem. June consumer prices fell on the month for the first time since 2020, headline inflation slowed to 3.5% from a year earlier against expectations of 3.8%, and core inflation rose only 2.6%. A subsequent producer-price reading also undershot expectations, even as oil remained elevated by the renewed US-Iran conflict. Those releases broke the momentum behind an increasingly crowded call for a Federal Reserve rate increase in July. The probability assigned to a move this month, which had approached 40%-50% before the inflation figures, fell below 17%, and traders shifted the likely start of any tightening toward September or October.

The resulting Treasury rally was powerful but deliberately front-loaded. The two-year yield closed at 4.18%, down 8 basis points after touching roughly 4.14% intraday, while the five-year fell 6 basis points to 4.31%. The 10-year declined only 4 basis points to 4.58%, and the 30-year lost 2 basis points to finish at 5.08%. That pattern steepened the 2s10s curve by 4 basis points to 40 basis points and the 2s30s curve by 6 basis points to 90 basis points. The market therefore removed a near-term policy premium, but it did not grant the long end equal relief from fiscal supply, oil-related inflation risk, or uncertainty about where nominal growth will settle.

This distinction is the session’s central fixed-income message. Softer inflation bought the Fed time; it did not create a conventional easing cycle. Chairman Kevin Warsh said the inflation mission was not accomplished and acknowledged that interest rates remain among the tools that may ultimately be needed. At the same time, Brent ended at $85.30, up 2.40%, and WTI at $79.85, up 2.19%, preserving a plausible second-round challenge to the benign June readings. The 10-year breakeven slipped only 1 basis point to 2.25%, whereas the 10-year real yield fell 3 basis points to 2.33%. Most of the nominal rally was consequently a real-rate adjustment rather than a wholesale disappearance of inflation compensation.

Risk assets treated the repricing as a reduction in imminent policy error rather than a recession alarm. Investment-grade and high-yield bond exchange-traded funds gained modestly, Goldman Sachs drew about $32 billion of peak orders for a $10 billion high-grade sale, and equities rose as volatility declined. Yet the simultaneous gains in oil and gold showed that investors did not abandon geopolitical or inflation hedges. The close was therefore constructive for carry and near-intermediate duration, but less decisive for long-duration sovereign risk. The most attractive interpretation is not “inflation defeated,” but “near-term tightening delayed while term and supply premia remain structurally expensive.”

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

The rates move began as an unwind of a very specific position: the expectation that persistent inflation and the energy shock could force the Fed to raise rates at its July meeting. Before the release, options markets had lifted the chance of a quarter-point increase to about one-half from less than 10%, and the two-year yield had traded above 4.25%. The June CPI report challenged the timing of that thesis. A monthly decline in headline prices, little movement in the core index, and the largest sequential fall in core services excluding housing since the pandemic provided enough evidence for investors to stop treating an immediate hike as the base case. The softer producer-price result reinforced the adjustment later in the session.

The Treasury curve shows precisely where confidence improved and where it did not. Two-year yields fell twice as much as 10-year yields and four times as much as 30-year yields. The five-year sector also outperformed the long bond, while the 2s5s10s butterfly held near minus 14 basis points. This is consistent with a market that sees less urgency for the Fed to act in July but still demands compensation beyond the policy horizon. The 2s10s30s butterfly moved 2 basis points to minus 10 basis points, another indication that the long end resisted the rally. A parallel move would have implied a broad reassessment of inflation and growth; the observed bull steepening instead isolated the relief around the expected path of policy.

The bill curve makes the same point at higher resolution. Four-week and eight-week coupon-equivalent yields were essentially unchanged at 3.70% and 3.72%, and the six-week yield rose 2 basis points to 3.72%. These maturities remain anchored to immediate cash conditions and the current policy setting. Farther out, the 13- and 17-week yields each fell 5 basis points to 3.80% and 3.85%, the 26-week yield dropped 8 basis points to 3.92%, and the 52-week yield declined 10 basis points to 4.02%. The repricing became larger as maturities captured more Fed meetings. Investors did not price an abrupt change in overnight conditions; they reduced the premium for a sequence of hikes over the balance of the year.

Real yields confirm that the rally was meaningful for financial conditions. Five-, seven-, and 10-year TIPS real yields each declined 3 basis points, ending at 2.03%, 2.17%, and 2.33%. The 20- and 30-year real yields fell only 2 basis points to 2.67% and 2.87%. The persistence of exceptionally positive long real yields matters for mortgages, capital expenditure, leveraged borrowers, and equity valuation even when the Fed pauses. It also explains why the market could celebrate a cooler inflation print while retaining a steep upward slope across the real-yield curve. The economy received marginal relief at the front and belly, but long-horizon financing remains restrictive.

Inflation compensation was notably stable relative to the size of the energy move. The 10-year breakeven closed at 2.25%, down just 1 basis point, despite Brent’s 2.40% rise and renewed concern about fuel supply through the Strait of Hormuz. That restraint suggests investors gave substantial weight to the actual June inflation readings and to the decision to abandon a proposed 20% cargo charge through the strait. It does not mean the oil shock is irrelevant. June inflation predates much of the latest rise in crude and refined products, so the market still has to distinguish a one-time level effect from broader pass-through into transport, goods, wages, and expectations.

Warsh’s testimony kept that distinction alive. He declined to signal an immediate increase, but said price stability had not been secured and indicated that the Federal Open Market Committee would have to debate the timing and extent of using its tools. That combination supports patience without validating a dovish pivot. It also explains why the market moved toward pricing a hike fully only by December rather than removing further tightening from the curve altogether. The Fed now has room to wait, but the threshold for declaring victory remains high while annual headline inflation is 3.5% and energy supply is uncertain.

For duration positioning, the close favored the intermediate sector over an indiscriminate extension into the long bond. The front end had carried the most acute meeting risk and therefore enjoyed the cleanest relief. The belly benefited from lower real yields and a delayed policy path. The 30-year sector, by contrast, still has to absorb fiscal issuance and a real yield near 2.9%, while guarding against renewed inflation pass-through. Bull steepening was not an incidental shape change; it was the market’s judgment that a cooler month can postpone policy action more easily than it can erase long-run term compensation.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Credit markets accepted the rates rally as supportive but did not display the kind of spread compression that would justify a broad risk-on conclusion. The LQD investment-grade bond fund rose 0.23%, while HYG and JNK gained 0.20% and 0.15%. Those moves were positive but modest next to the shift in front-end Treasury yields. They indicate that lower risk-free rates improved total-return conditions without prompting investors to ignore oil risk, elevated real yields, or rich valuations. The result was a carry-friendly session, not a repricing of borrower fundamentals.

Primary issuance supplied the strongest direct evidence that market access remained open. Goldman Sachs raised $10 billion after its second-quarter results, and the offering attracted roughly $32 billion of orders at its peak. Demand of more than three times the deal size is a meaningful absorption signal on a day when the policy outlook changed abruptly. Investors were willing to add high-grade financial exposure even as the issuer brought substantial size, and the inflation surprise lowered the immediate rate penalty for doing so. The transaction also benefited from strong operating results, including a 32% annual increase in fixed-income, currency, and commodities revenue, so its success should not be generalized to weaker borrowers without qualification.

Municipal bonds offered the counterexample to effortless demand. Top-rated 10-year municipals yielded only 65.5% of comparable Treasuries as of July 10, versus a five-year average of 71%, while long municipals were near their richest relative valuation against corporate debt in three years. Months of inflows had improved technical conditions, but the resulting price levels were beginning to make institutional investors more selective. This matters because a benign Treasury day can conceal deteriorating relative value: an asset may deliver positive total return while offering too little compensation for incremental duration, liquidity, or tax-policy uncertainty.

Funding conditions also remained differentiated by horizon. The shortest Treasury bills barely moved, suggesting no disruption in immediate liquidity, whereas six- to 12-month yields fell materially as investors removed some tightening premium. For banks and high-grade issuers, that combination is constructive: cash markets remain orderly and expected term funding costs eased. For lower-quality or highly leveraged borrowers, however, the absolute level of real yields remains demanding, and a delayed Fed hike is not equivalent to cheap money. The session improved refinancing arithmetic at the margin but did not reverse the restrictive financing regime.

The proper credit conclusion is therefore selective resilience. Strong demand for a large bank transaction, modest gains in both investment-grade and high-yield funds, and a lower front-end rate path argue against immediate funding stress. Rich municipal valuations and persistently high long real yields argue against chasing duration or compression simply because CPI surprised lower. Credit can continue to earn carry if inflation remains contained, but dispersion should increase wherever issuers depend on long-dated funding, energy-sensitive cash flows, or repeated market access.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Japan produced the clearest non-US duration rally. Twenty- and 30-year Japanese government bond yields fell as much as 18 basis points to 3.565% and 3.725%, while the 40-year yield declined 10 basis points. The move combined a strong 20-year auction with Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama’s suggestion that government bonds could be added to tax-advantaged individual savings accounts. The proposal opened a credible route for domestic household savings to support sovereign demand and potentially the yen, while comments about the Government Pension Investment Fund encouraged speculation about larger institutional purchases.

The scale is potentially significant but should not be mistaken for an announced allocation change. The ¥293.6 trillion pension fund held 26.9% of assets in domestic bonds in March and can deviate above its 25% benchmark within an existing band. An estimate that moving to 31% could generate as much as ¥12.3 trillion, or $76 billion, of additional JGB demand illustrates the market’s sensitivity to marginal buyers. Yet expectations of a near-term strategic overhaul were subsequently tempered. The actual auction strength was firm evidence; the pension-flow estimate was a scenario. That difference matters after an 18-basis-point long-end rally.

Europe and the United Kingdom faced the opposite impulse. The rise in oil caused traders to accelerate expectations for Bank of England and European Central Bank tightening after starting July with less than one quarter-point of increases priced through the following year. Markets moved to expect a 25-basis-point ECB increase in September and treated another move by year-end as highly likely. The mechanism is straightforward: an energy shock raises headline inflation directly, weakens real household income, and can still force central banks to tighten if expectations or wages respond. That is an unfavorable mix for duration because growth protection and inflation control point in different directions.

UK fiscal risk added a term-premium channel beyond monetary policy. Long-dated gilt exposure had already been reduced in some multi-asset portfolios amid concern that a new government led by Andy Burnham could loosen fiscal policy. Internal inflation projections were reportedly lifted to 3.2% for the fourth quarter, while higher borrowing costs had already reduced a £23.6 billion fiscal-rule buffer by at least £5.5 billion. The lesson from the gilt market is that fiscal space is endogenous to yields: expectations of more borrowing lift debt-service costs, which narrow the buffer and can demand still more term premium. Oil magnifies that loop by worsening inflation and nominal funding costs.

Australia provided a smaller but useful illustration of the same tension. Consumer sentiment rose 4.1% to 83.9 as fuel prices eased, yet the index remained in the bottom decile of its 50-year history. The Reserve Bank of Australia had held its policy rate at 4.35% after raising it at its first three meetings of the year, and renewed energy inflation complicates any improvement in household confidence. A single rebound in sentiment therefore does not remove the pressure on family finances or grant the central bank room to look through imported prices.

Across developed markets, duration performance was governed by the identity of the prospective buyer and the source of inflation. Japan rallied because domestic-demand initiatives and a strong auction improved the absorption story. UK and euro-area duration faced renewed tightening expectations because oil worsened the inflation trade-off. US Treasuries occupied the middle ground: softer realized inflation dominated the front end, while fiscal and energy risks constrained the long end. The global message is that sovereign curves are no longer moving on a single inflation factor; buyer composition, issuance credibility, and fiscal transmission increasingly determine how much relief a policy repricing can deliver.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging-market debt ended modestly firmer, with the EMB fund up 0.20%, helped by the combination of lower US yields and a 0.38% decline in the dollar index to 100.894. That is a constructive external backdrop because it reduces the immediate dollar-discount-rate pressure on hard-currency bonds and can ease currency stress. The benefit was limited, however, by the simultaneous increase in oil. For importers, a weaker dollar does not fully offset a 2%-plus rise in crude, particularly when domestic inflation expectations and subsidy costs are already sensitive to fuel.

Indonesia captured that trade-off. S&P’s affirmation of the sovereign rating provided an institutional vote of confidence after the domestic bond selloff in June, and the finance ministry argued that it should help stabilize the market. Yet the 10-year government yield still rose 3 basis points to 7.26% as investors focused on oil and its inflation consequences. The divergence between positive rating news and weaker bond performance shows that near-term macro terms of trade can dominate a stable medium-term credit assessment. It also explains why local investors favored short- to intermediate maturities, where yields were considered compelling without taking the full duration exposure of the long end.

Debt management becomes more important in that environment. Indonesia emphasized a prudent, flexible, and measured issuance strategy, language that matters when global real yields are high and commodity shocks can alter foreign demand quickly. Sovereigns that can adjust tenor, maintain investor communication, and avoid concentrating refinancing needs are better placed to preserve access. Those with large external funding requirements or heavy energy imports remain exposed even if US policy tightening is delayed.

The US curve offered partial relief but not a clean all-clear. A lower two-year yield and softer dollar reduce the near-term hurdle for emerging-market assets, while the 30-year Treasury at 5.08% and the 30-year real yield at 2.87% keep the global long-duration benchmark restrictive. The result favors carry in sovereigns with credible policy and manageable issuance over broad duration extension. Dollar liquidity improved at the margin on July 14; the cost of long-term capital did not fall enough to eliminate refinancing or debt-management risk.

## Cross asset

Cross-asset prices reinforced the view that the inflation surprise reduced immediate Fed risk without removing geopolitical hedging. The S&P 500 rose 0.38% to 7,543.59, the Nasdaq 100 gained 1.10% to 29,586.29, and the semiconductor index advanced 2.54% to 12,661.93. The VIX fell 3.85% to 16.5. Growth and technology shares benefited most because the fall in real yields improved the present value of long-dated earnings, while the lower probability of a July hike reduced the chance of an abrupt policy shock.

The dollar index declined 0.38% to 100.894 as the US front end rallied, a coherent response to the removal of near-term tightening premium. Gold nevertheless climbed 1.55% to $4,058.80. Bullion’s gain alongside a weaker dollar and lower real yields is mechanically consistent, but it also signaled continued demand for protection against conflict, inflation volatility, and policy uncertainty. Gold had remained near $4,000 after a difficult second quarter, and the July 14 close showed how quickly softer US inflation could restore support even while oil posed a separate inflation threat.

Energy was the critical counterweight. WTI rose 2.19% to $79.85 and Brent gained 2.40% to $85.30 amid renewed disruption concerns around the Strait of Hormuz. The decision not to pursue a 20% cargo fee reduced one extreme tail risk, but the underlying conflict still lifted crude and refined-product prices. This created an unusual but intelligible combination: bonds and equities rallied on cooler backward-looking inflation, while oil and gold rose on forward uncertainty. Investors accepted that the Fed could wait, but continued to pay for protection against the possibility that June’s improvement will prove temporary.

Credit’s smaller gains fit between those poles. LQD rose 0.23%, HYG 0.20%, and JNK 0.15%, all positive but far below the semiconductor rally. That relative performance suggests the rates move did more for long-duration equity valuation than for the perceived fundamental quality of leveraged borrowers. It also cautions against reading the equity close as a broad declaration of macro safety. The session rewarded exposure to lower real rates, but kept a premium on assets that hedge energy and geopolitical outcomes.

Taken together, the cross-asset map validates the Treasury curve’s message. The dominant shock was a reduction in imminent Fed tightening, visible in the weaker dollar, lower real yields, technology outperformance, and lower volatility. The residual risk was an oil-driven return of inflation, visible in stronger crude and gold and in the long end’s limited rally. Neither signal cancels the other; the market closed with more time before the next policy move, but not with greater certainty about the eventual destination of rates.

## Forward Market Significance

The first question is whether July’s policy repricing survives the next inflation and energy evidence. A probability below 17% for a July increase is far less aggressive than the 40%-50% range seen before CPI, but traders still expect that tightening could begin in September or October and fully price a move only by December. Warsh’s insistence that the inflation mission is unfinished prevents a soft monthly print from becoming a promise to hold indefinitely. Upcoming readings must show whether the fall in core services and goods prices persists after the latest increase in oil.

The second question is where an energy shock enters the economy. June CPI did not capture much of the renewed rise in crude, and producer prices benefited from lower energy costs during the measured period. If Brent stays in the mid-$80s or moves higher, gasoline, diesel, aviation, shipping, and goods distribution will become the transmission points to monitor. A contained, one-time increase would allow the Fed to remain patient; broader pass-through into expectations and wages would revive the hike debate. The 10-year breakeven near 2.25% currently assumes the latter can be avoided, so a material rise in inflation compensation would be an early sign that the benign interpretation is weakening.

The third question is whether the long end can participate more fully in any further rally. The 2s30s steepening to 90 basis points and the 30-year yield at 5.08% show that delaying a Fed move does not solve fiscal supply or term-premium concerns. Continued front-end outperformance would keep the curve steepening and could eventually tighten long-term borrowing conditions even as expected policy rates decline. A more durable duration rally requires either repeated disinflation, clearer fiscal restraint, stronger auction demand, or some combination of all three.

Outside the US, announced liquidity and issuance plans will test sovereign absorption. The People’s Bank of China is set to inject 1.4 trillion yuan through a six-month outright reverse-repurchase operation as tax payments and debt sales approach. Net central- and local-government issuance is estimated at 4.2 trillion to 4.4 trillion yuan in the third quarter after first-half issuance reached only about 52% of the annual target. The operation can smooth funding pressure, but record quarterly supply will reveal whether liquidity support is sufficient to stabilize yields without crowding out credit expansion.

Japan’s next test is whether policy suggestions become durable demand. Adding JGBs to tax-advantaged savings accounts could broaden the retail buyer base, and the pension fund has room within its existing bands to increase domestic bond holdings. Neither mechanism is yet equivalent to a committed ¥12.3 trillion purchase program. Auction performance, actual allocation disclosures, and yen behavior will determine whether the July 14 long-end rally reflected a lasting change in demand or an optimistic extrapolation from official comments.

The UK and euro area face a more difficult calendar because oil, fiscal policy, and central-bank expectations are moving together. Burnham is due to take office with higher inflation projections and a narrower fiscal buffer, so early budget signals will matter most at the long end of the gilt curve. ECB and BOE pricing will remain sensitive to whether energy inflation is treated as temporary or allowed to influence wages and expectations. The Bank of Canada, meanwhile, is widely expected to keep its overnight rate at 2.25% for a sixth meeting, balancing core inflation near target against a first-quarter contraction at a 0.1% annualized rate, far below its earlier 1.5% estimate.

For credit and emerging markets, the practical test is whether primary demand stays as strong when issuance broadens beyond highly rated banks. Goldman’s $10 billion transaction showed ample capacity, but rich municipal valuations and high long real yields argue that investors will remain price-sensitive. Indonesia’s rating affirmation similarly supports medium-term credibility, yet its 10-year yield’s rise to 7.26% shows that oil can overwhelm positive credit news over a single session. Deal concessions, orderbook quality, local-currency performance, and sovereign tenor choices will provide a cleaner measure of resilience than headline risk-asset gains alone.

The durable signal from July 14 is therefore conditional relief. Cooler inflation weakened the case for an immediate Fed hike, lowered real yields, supported risk assets, and eased the dollar. Oil, fiscal supply, and hawkish central-bank language kept long-duration compensation elevated. If subsequent inflation releases remain soft despite the energy shock, the bull-steepening move can extend and credit carry can remain supported. If oil passes through or fiscal absorption deteriorates, the front-end rally will look less like the start of a broad duration cycle and more like a temporary postponement of tightening.

section_summaries.market_read: Conditional bull steepening favors carry and intermediate duration over an indiscriminate long-bond extension.
section_summaries.next_check: Whether subsequent inflation data remain soft after the latest energy rise and whether the July-hike probability stays below 17%.
section_summaries.summary: The market removed a large portion of the near-term Fed tightening premium after softer inflation, but did not embrace a conventional easing cycle. Relief concentrated in the front end and belly while the long end remained constrained by oil, fiscal issuance, and elevated real yields.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The close distinguishes a delay in tightening from a durable defeat of inflation, leaving long-horizon financing conditions restrictive even as immediate policy risk recedes.

section_summaries.market_read: The two-year at 4.18% and five-year at 4.31% offer the cleanest expression of delayed tightening; the 30-year at 5.08% still carries substantial supply and inflation risk.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch 10-year breakevens near 2.25%, long real yields, auction demand, and whether Fed communication preserves patience without validating a dovish pivot.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: The Treasury move was a targeted unwind of imminent-hike risk: yields fell most where maturities captured the next several Fed meetings, while cash conditions at the shortest bill maturities barely changed. Real yields declined across the curve, but stable breakevens and limited long-end participation showed that inflation and term compensation remained embedded.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: A bull steepener eases near-term financial conditions without delivering equivalent relief to mortgages, capital spending, and other long-duration borrowing costs.

section_summaries.market_read: Carry conditions improved, but small gains in LQD, HYG, and JNK point to resilience rather than a major repricing of borrower fundamentals.
section_summaries.next_check: Test whether strong orderbooks persist as issuance broadens beyond highly rated banks and whether deal concessions begin to rise.
section_summaries.summary: Credit benefited modestly from lower risk-free rates, while strong demand for Goldman Sachs's $10 billion high-grade sale confirmed that large, well-regarded issuers retained market access. Rich municipal valuations and high long real yields kept the signal selective rather than broadly risk-on.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Open primary markets reduce immediate refinancing stress, yet weaker borrowers and long-duration buyers still face restrictive absolute yields and limited valuation cushions.

section_summaries.market_read: JGB strength has a tangible auction foundation but still prices optimistic demand scenarios; gilts and European rates remain exposed to the combination of energy inflation and fiscal pressure.
section_summaries.next_check: Look for actual Japanese retail and pension allocations, early UK fiscal signals, and whether ECB and BOE pricing continues to harden as oil feeds into inflation expectations.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Japan's long end rallied on a strong auction and prospects for broader domestic demand, while UK and euro-area duration faced renewed oil-driven tightening expectations. The divergence shows that buyer composition, fiscal credibility, and the origin of inflation increasingly determine sovereign-curve performance.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Sovereign duration can no longer be treated as a single global inflation trade when domestic absorption and debt-service feedback loops produce sharply different term-premium outcomes.

section_summaries.market_read: The setup favors carry in credible sovereigns with flexible issuance and manageable refinancing needs rather than broad long-duration exposure.
section_summaries.next_check: Monitor local-currency performance, oil sensitivity, issuance tenor, and whether sovereign funding plans preserve demand without concentrating refinancing risk.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: A softer dollar and lower US front-end yields gave emerging-market debt modest support, but higher oil limited the benefit for energy importers. Indonesia's rising 10-year yield despite a rating affirmation illustrated how near-term inflation and terms-of-trade pressure can overwhelm positive credit news.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Dollar liquidity improved at the margin, but a 5.08% US 30-year yield and elevated global real rates keep the long-term capital hurdle demanding.

section_summaries.market_read: The weaker dollar, rising equities, and lower VIX confirm reduced imminent policy risk, while oil and gold preserve a clear forward-inflation warning.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether oil holds near current levels, whether technology leadership broadens, and whether credit begins to outperform rather than merely track the rates rally.
section_summaries.summary: Technology shares outperformed and volatility fell as lower real yields reduced the threat of an abrupt Fed move. At the same time, stronger crude and gold showed that investors continued to hedge conflict, inflation volatility, and the risk that June's disinflation proves temporary.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The split explains why the Treasury rally remained front-loaded and why credit gains lagged the equity response despite an apparently favorable macro shock.

section_summaries.market_read: The base case is more time before the next policy move, not certainty that rates have reached their final destination.
section_summaries.next_check: Track the next inflation prints, energy-sensitive transport and goods prices, US long-end auctions, China's liquidity-versus-supply test, and upcoming central-bank decisions.
section_summaries.summary: The durability of the relief depends on whether energy pass-through remains contained, the long end attracts stronger demand, and sovereign issuance can be absorbed without renewed term-premium pressure. Fed patience can extend the bull steepener, but later-year tightening remains plausible if oil lifts wages, expectations, or core inflation.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Repeated disinflation could sustain duration and credit carry, whereas energy pass-through or weaker fiscal absorption would recast the rally as a temporary postponement of tightening.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.33% (-3 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.25% (-1 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 40 bp (+4 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 90 bp (+6 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Cooler Inflation Delays the Hike, but Oil and Term Premium Keep the Long End Heavy
overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: Below-expected CPI and PPI readings broke the momentum behind the July-hike trade and shifted tightening expectations toward later in the year. Two-year yields fell 8 basis points to 4.18%, compared with declines of 4 basis points in the 10-year and 2 basis points in the 30-year, steepening 2s10s to 40 basis points and 2s30s to 90 basis points. Lower real yields and reduced policy-error risk supported technology shares, credit, and emerging-market debt while weakening the dollar; simultaneous gains in oil and gold kept geopolitical and inflation hedges firmly in demand.
overall_summary.summary: Soft June inflation sharply reduced the risk of an immediate Fed hike, driving a front-loaded Treasury rally, lower real yields, a weaker dollar, and firmer risk assets. The relief remained conditional: crude and gold rose, the 30-year yield held above 5%, and the curve steepened as investors preserved compensation for energy pass-through, fiscal supply, and long-run inflation uncertainty.

watch_events.challenges_if: Energy-sensitive prices, wages, or inflation expectations reaccelerate.
watch_events.confirms_if: Core services and goods remain soft while inflation compensation stays contained.
watch_events.date: Next scheduled US CPI and PPI releases
watch_events.market_implication: Would reinforce delayed Fed tightening and support front-end and intermediate duration.
watch_events.market_trigger: Another soft CPI and PPI set with contained energy pass-through.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Fed pricing, two-year Treasuries, real yields, and the dollar.
watch_events.title: Next US inflation releases
watch_events.watch_line: Test whether June's softer core-services and goods readings persist after crude's latest rise.
watch_events.why_it_matters: June's data postponed the July hike debate but predated much of the latest oil increase.

watch_events.challenges_if: Pass-through broadens into transport, goods distribution, wages, or expectations.
watch_events.confirms_if: Fuel gains remain a contained level effect and breakevens stay near 2.25%.
watch_events.market_implication: Would revive hike risk, pressure the long end, and weaken the conditional duration rally.
watch_events.market_trigger: Brent holding in the mid-$80s or higher alongside a material rise in inflation compensation.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Breakevens, Fed pricing, long-end Treasuries, credit, and the dollar.
watch_events.title: Oil pass-through and inflation expectations
watch_events.watch_line: Track gasoline, diesel, aviation, shipping, wages, and the 10-year breakeven near 2.25%.
watch_events.why_it_matters: The latest crude rise was not fully captured in June inflation data.

watch_events.challenges_if: Weak demand or larger concessions keep the 30-year yield above 5%.
watch_events.confirms_if: Bid quality stays firm and the long end participates more fully in the rally.
watch_events.market_implication: Would broaden the rally beyond the front end and reduce term-premium pressure.
watch_events.market_trigger: Strong auction absorption and a sustained move in the 30-year yield below 5%.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Thirty-year Treasuries, curve steepness, mortgages, and long-duration credit.
watch_events.title: US long-end auction demand
watch_events.watch_line: Assess whether fiscal issuance finds enough demand for the 30-year yield to hold below 5%.
watch_events.why_it_matters: Delayed Fed tightening does not by itself resolve fiscal-supply or long-run inflation risk.

watch_events.challenges_if: Supply overwhelms liquidity, lifts yields, or crowds out credit creation.
watch_events.confirms_if: Yields remain stable and private credit expansion is not displaced by government borrowing.
watch_events.market_implication: Successful absorption would steady Chinese rates; strain would tighten funding and pressure duration.
watch_events.market_trigger: Liquidity support absorbs record government supply without destabilizing yields or crowding out credit.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Chinese sovereign yields, money markets, credit growth, and regional duration.
watch_events.title: China liquidity versus government issuance
watch_events.watch_line: Compare the 1.4 trillion yuan liquidity operation with 4.2-4.4 trillion yuan of estimated quarterly issuance.
watch_events.why_it_matters: The funding operation smooths tax and debt-sale pressure but covers only part of expected supply.

watch_events.challenges_if: Japanese flows fail to materialize or European energy inflation and fiscal pressure ease.
watch_events.confirms_if: JGB auctions and allocation disclosures show real demand as European tightening expectations persist.
watch_events.market_implication: Would support JGBs while keeping gilt and euro-area duration under pressure.
watch_events.market_trigger: Durable Japanese allocations alongside firmer ECB and BOE hike pricing as oil feeds inflation risk.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: JGB long yields, the yen, gilts, euro-area rates, and central-bank pricing.
watch_events.title: Japan demand and European policy repricing
watch_events.watch_line: Verify actual Japanese household and pension JGB demand while tracking energy-led UK and euro-area repricing.
watch_events.why_it_matters: Buyer composition supports Japan, while energy and fiscal risk work against European duration.
